paper_title: Using a composite flow law to model deformation in the NEEM deep ice core, Greenland – Part 1: The role of grain size and grain size distribution on deformation of the upper 2207 m

methods.description: This method involves analyzing the microstructure of the NEEM deep ice core using light microscope images to determine the evolution of grain size with depth.

steps.description: The NEEM deep ice core was chosen because a comprehensive light microscope dataset was available.
steps.searchable_summary: access light microscope dataset for NEEM deep ice core

steps.description: These LM images were made of sections that were cut parallel to the long (vertical) axis of the ice core.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare ice core sections for imaging

steps.description: Each LM image is about 90 mm long and about 55 mm wide and was digitally analyzed using the Ice-image software.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze LM images using Ice-image software

steps.description: A lower cutoff grain size diameter of 0.3 mm was used to exclude small features.
steps.searchable_summary: apply cutoff to exclude small grain features

steps.description: Grains with grain boundaries that were interrupted by the edge of the sample were excluded from the grain size data.

methods.description: This method compares the microscale constant stress model and the microscale constant strain rate model to understand their effects on grain deformation rates.

steps.description: The main difference between the microscale constant stress model and the microscale constant strain rate model is that the microscale constant stress model allows the smallest grains to deform more than an order of magnitude faster than the larger grains.
steps.searchable_summary: compare deformation rates in microscale models

steps.description: For the microscale constant strain rate model, the strain rate is set to be the same for each grain size class.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze strain rate uniformity in strain rate model

steps.description: The average strain rate in the Holocene ice calculated using the microscale constant stress model is 60% higher than the average strain rate produced by the microscale constant strain rate model.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate and compare average strain rates

methods.description: This method compares the strain rates calculated using a grain size distribution model and a mean grain size model to evaluate their computational efficiency and accuracy.

steps.description: The difference in calculated strain rate between using a grain size distribution with the microscale constant strain rate model and the mean grain size model is relatively small.
steps.searchable_summary: compare strain rates in grain size distribution and mean grain size models

steps.description: The much larger computational expense of the microscale constant strain rate model and the small difference in calculated strain with the mean grain size model argues for using a mean grain size model.
The strain rate peaks predicted in the layers at about 1980 m and 2100 m depth are 2 to 3 times larger for the microscale constant stress model compared to the mean grain size model.
steps.searchable_summary: predict strain rate peaks in models
